---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (каротажная диаграмма)** — a composite well log typical of Soviet-era mineral and petroleum exploration. It combines multiple geophysical logging curves plotted against depth, alongside a lithological column and a data table. A smaller supplementary log panel appears at the bottom of the sheet (labeled "Appendix/Приложение").

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log appears to cover a **single deep borehole** (скважина), though the specific borehole number is partially illegible due to scan quality — it may be in the range of **скв-1** or a similarly numbered hole. The depth range appears to span from approximately **0 to ~1,300–1,400 metres** based on the length of the curves and the visible tick marks along the depth axis on the left margin. The figure belongs to the regional survey of **northern Kazakhstan** (map sheets M-42 / N-42, 1:200,000 scale). Formation names and stratigraphic horizon labels are visible along the left-hand lithological column but are largely illegible at this resolution.

---

## Key Content

The composite log contains the following identifiable components, arranged in vertical columns from left to right:

1. **Depth scale column (левая шкала глубин):** A vertical depth axis in metres, running top to bottom. Tick marks appear at regular intervals (likely every 10 or 20 m). Depth values are partially readable — values in the range of approximately **200–1,300 m** are suggested by the scale length.

2. **Lithological column (литологическая колонка):** A narrow strip on the left side showing rock types through standard Soviet lithological symbols:
   - **Diagonal hatching** (upper section) — likely crystalline basement or dense rock (granite/gneiss)
   - **Dotted patterns** — sandstone or sandy intervals
   - **Brick-pattern hatching** — limestone or carbonate horizons
   - **Horizontal lines** — clay or shale intervals
   - Mixed symbols suggest interbedded sequences

3. **Geophysical logging curves (каротажные кривые):** The central portion of the figure shows two or three overlapping waveform traces plotted against depth. These are the dominant visual feature — large amplitude, irregular curves spanning roughly the middle third of the sheet width. Based on Soviet logging conventions, these likely represent:
   - **КС (кажущееся сопротивление / apparent resistivity)** — the higher-amplitude, more variable curve
   - **ПС (потенциал самополяризации / spontaneous potential / SP)** — a smoother curve running alongside
   - Possibly a **gamma-ray (ГК)** or **neutron log** curve in the third trace
   - A notable zone of **high-amplitude deflections** occupies roughly the upper-to-middle depth interval (~200–700 m), with deflections becoming lower-amplitude and more subdued in the lower portion (~700–1,300 m), possibly indicating a transition from more resistive (volcanic? carbonate?) to less resistive (clastic? altered?) lithologies.

4. **Right-hand data table:** A multi-column tabular section on the right side of the sheet contains numerical values arranged by depth interval. Column headers are illegible but likely include: **core recovery (%), interval, lithology code, sample numbers, geochemical or physical property values**. Values visible in cells appear to be single- or double-digit integers and decimals, consistent with core recovery percentages or porosity/density values.

5. **Stratigraphic/horizon markers:** Horizontal lines cutting across the log at several depths mark **stratigraphic boundaries or formation tops**, with labels on the left margin (largely illegible but appear to include Cyrillic abbreviations for formation names or age designations — possibly Палеозой/Paleozoic subdivisions).

6. **Supplementary panel (bottom):** A smaller log panel labeled "**Адонис**" (or similar) shows a shorter depth interval with additional curves — possibly a detailed blowup of a specific interval of interest, or logs from a related hole.
